sections.title: What relative clauses do (core logic)
sections.content:
A **relative clause** adds information about a noun without starting a new sentence — the relative pronoun links the two ideas together.
- The Clause Linking root (#4 in the Tree Model): English **embeds** extra information inside a noun phrase instead of breaking it into short choppy sentences — what makes B1/B2 writing sound like adult prose instead of beginner sentences.
- Key principle: the relative pronoun replaces the noun *inside* the second clause — so it sits at the start of the relative clause, not in its original position.

items.label: person
items.formula: The man is a doctor. + He lives next door. → The man **who lives next door** is a doctor.

items.label: thing
items.formula: The book is excellent. + I'm reading it. → The book **that I'm reading** is excellent.

diagrams.text: Czech often uses *který/která/které* for all of these; English distributes the same job across **who, which, that, whose, where, when, why**, with rules that decide which fits.

sections.title: The relative pronouns — who / which / that / whose
sections.content: **The core four** — most relative clauses use one of these. The choice depends on **what kind of noun** you're adding to, and (for *that*) **whether the clause is defining**.

diagrams.title: who / which / that / whose at a glance
rows: Subject or object; works in both defining and non-defining clauses
Works in both defining and non-defining clauses
**Defining clauses only** — never after a comma

items.good: The teacher **who** taught us French has retired.
The car **which** I bought is red.
Anything **that** moves attracts the cat.
items.why: people OR things — but only in defining clauses

items.good: That's the writer **whose** books I love.
the man **who** lives there
items.why: pick the wrong one and the sentence is instantly marked as L1-interference

sections.title: Defining vs non-defining (the comma rule)
sections.content:
**The single highest-impact distinction in B2 writing.** Commas are not decoration — they change the meaning.
In exams and emails, the comma signals whether the information is critical or parenthetical.

right.title: Non-defining — commas
points: Extra info: the sentence still identifies the noun without it

diagrams.title: Defining vs non-defining side-by-side
rows: *The book **that I bought** is good.* (which book? the one I bought)
*This book, **which I bought yesterday**, is good.* (just one book — extra info added)
*The man **who lives next door** is a doctor.* (which man?)
*My brother, **who lives in Brno**, is a doctor.* (one brother — location is extra)
*Cities **that have metros** are easier to get around.* (only some cities)
*Prague, **which is the capital**, has a metro.* (one Prague — capital is extra)

diagrams.text: *My brother who lives in Brno* (defining — I have several brothers, this one) is a different claim from *My brother, who lives in Brno,* (non-defining — I have one brother, and by the way he lives in Brno).

sections.title: Contact clauses — when you can drop the pronoun
sections.content:
In **defining clauses** only, you can omit the relative pronoun **if it's the object** of the relative clause — a **contact clause**, extremely common in natural English.
Dropping the pronoun where you can sounds fluent rather than over-formal; keeping it where you can't is the marker of careful English.

steps.q: Is the next word after the gap a verb? (pronoun = subject)

steps.end: new subject + verb follows → it's the object — drop it freely

items.good: The book **(that) I bought** is good.
items.why: *that* is the object of *I bought __* → can drop

items.good: The man **(who) I met** yesterday called me.
items.why: *who* is the object of *I met __* → can drop

items.good: The man **who lives** next door is a doctor.
items.why: *who* is the subject of *__ lives* → must keep

items.good: The book **that fell** off the shelf is mine.

sections.title: Whose, where, when, why
sections.content:
Beyond *who/which/that*, four more relatives cover **possession, place, time and reason** — each replaces a longer *preposition + which* construction.
These let you build single tight sentences out of two — exactly the move CAE/FCE Use of English transformations test, and what good writing actually does.

diagrams.title: The four
rows: The student **whose essay** won the prize is here. / A building **whose roof** is leaking.
The town **where I grew up** is small. (= the town **in which** I grew up)
The year **when I was born** was 1982. (= the year **in which** I was born)
The reason **why he left** is unclear. (= the reason **for which** he left)

items.label: where / at which
items.formula: The hotel **where we stayed** was excellent. (natural) / The hotel **at which we stayed** was excellent. (formal)

items.label: when / (that)
items.formula: I remember the day **when we met**. / I remember the day **(that) we met**. (both fine; *when* is slightly more natural here)

sections.title: Preposition + which / whom (formal)
sections.content:
When the relative pronoun is the **object of a preposition**, English gives you two options — the single most visible register marker in English relatives.
Job applications, academic essays and contracts use *preposition + whom/which*; everyday speech and email don't. Choosing wrongly makes you sound either stiff or careless for the context.

right.title: Formal — preposition + whom (people) / which (things)
points: The report **to which I am referring**.

diagrams.text: Czech keeps the preposition with the relative pronoun (*ke kterému, na kterém*). English's habit of **stranding the preposition** at the end (*the person I spoke **to***) feels strange at first but is the **default natural English** — embrace it. Use *whom/which* with a fronted preposition only when the register actually calls for it.

items.good: the report **to which** I'm referring
items.bad: the report to that I'm referring
items.why: never front a preposition with *that* — must be *which* or *whom*

sections.title: Advanced features (B2)
sections.content:
Three constructions that mark B2-level relative clauses — they appear in FCE/CAE reading and Use of English, and in any serious writing. They let you compress information further still.
Reduced relatives are how natural English keeps noun phrases short — recognise them when reading, deploy them when writing.

diagrams.title: 1. Quantifier + of whom / of which — non-defining, a portion of a group

items.label: all of whom
items.formula: I have three sisters, **all of whom** live abroad.

items.label: some of which
items.formula: He gave me ten books, **some of which** I had already read.

items.label: many of whom
items.formula: The candidates, **many of whom** were nervous, did well.

diagrams.title: 2. what as a relative — = the thing(s) that; starts its own clause with no noun before it

items.good: I didn't understand **what you said**. (= the thing that you said)
**What surprises me** is his calm. (= the thing that surprises me)
the book **that/which** I read

diagrams.title: 3. Reduced relative clauses — drop who/which + be (state or action)

items.label: -ing
items.formula: the man **sitting** there = the man **who is sitting** there

items.label: participle
items.formula: the book **written** by Kafka = the book **which was written** by Kafka

items.label: adjective
items.formula: anyone **interested** in the job should apply = anyone **who is interested**…

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors
sections.content:
**High-frequency mistakes** — mostly L1 interference from *který/co*. These five or six errors account for the vast majority of relative-clause mistakes in Czech-speaker writing; fixing them is one of the highest-leverage moves at B1→B2.
For more on commas with non-defining clauses, see the **Punctuation** topic.

items.good: the man **who/that** lives there
the woman **who/that** lives next door
items.bad: the woman which lives next door

items.good: Prague, **which** is the capital
Everything **that** he said
the man **whose** car / the man **who's** coming
items.why: whose = possession; who's = who is/has — don't mix them up

items.good: the book **(that) I read** — or simply: the book **I read**
My father, who is a doctor, lives in Brno.
items.bad: My father who is a doctor lives in Brno.
items.why: missing commas in non-defining — reads as one of several fathers!

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: The book that I read was interesting.
options.b: The book what I read was interesting.
options.c: The book what I read it was interesting.
options.d: The book I read what was interesting.

questions.explanation: We use **that** or **which** for things in a relative clause, not **what**.

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: The woman what lives next door is a doctor.
options.b: The woman what she lives next door is a doctor.
options.c: The woman who lives next door is a doctor.
options.d: The woman which lives next door is a doctor.

questions.explanation: We use **who** (or **that**) for people, not **what** or **which**.

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: This is the town what I was born.
options.b: This is the town what I was born in.
options.c: This is the town who I was born.
options.d: This is the town where I was born.

questions.explanation: For places we use **where** (or 'the town that/which I was born in'), not **what**.

questions.question: When is 'what' correct in a relative sense?

options.a: What I need is more time.
options.b: The film what we saw was good.
options.c: The thing what I said was wrong.
options.d: The person what helped me left.

questions.explanation: **What** means 'the thing(s) that' and starts its own clause (What I need = The thing that I need). We don't use **what** after a noun.

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: The day what we met was sunny.
options.b: The day what we met on was sunny.
options.c: The day when we met was sunny.
options.d: The day who we met was sunny.

questions.explanation: For time we use **when** (or 'the day that we met'), not **what**.

questions.question: The book ____ I'm reading is by a famous author.
questions.explanation: For things we use **that** (or which), not who.

questions.question: The woman ____ lives next door is very kind.
questions.explanation: For people (subject) we use **who** (or that).

questions.question: This is the house ____ I was born.
questions.explanation: For place we use **where** (or that/which ... in).

questions.question: The man ____ I spoke to was the manager.
questions.explanation: For people (object) we can use **who** (or that).

questions.question: I don't remember the day ____ we first met.
questions.explanation: For time we use **when** (or that), not what.

questions.question: The person ____ phone was stolen called the police.
questions.explanation: For possession we use **whose**.

questions.question: The town ____ I grew up is very small.
questions.explanation: For place we use **where** (or that/which ... in).

questions.question: The hotel ____ we stayed was very comfortable.
questions.explanation: For place we use **where** (or that/which ... at).

questions.question: Everything ____ he said was true.
questions.explanation: After everything/something/anything we use **that** (or nothing).

questions.question: The reason ____ I'm late is the traffic.
questions.explanation: For reason we use **why** (or that).

gapfill.title: Practice: Choose the correct relative pronoun

questions.question: The book ____ I'm reading is by a famous author.
answers: that
questions.explanation: For things we use **that** (or which), not **what**.

questions.question: The woman ____ lives next door is very kind.
answers: who
questions.explanation: For people (subject of the clause) we use **who** (or that).

questions.question: This is the house ____ I was born.
answers: where
questions.explanation: For place we use **where** (or 'that/which I was born in').

questions.question: The man ____ I spoke to was the manager.
answers: who
questions.explanation: For people (object) we use **who** (or that/whom); we can also omit: The man I spoke to.

questions.question: I don't remember the day ____ we first met.
answers: when
questions.explanation: For time we use **when** (or that), not **what**.

questions.question: The film ____ we saw yesterday was boring.
answers: that
questions.explanation: For things we use **that** or **which**, never **what** after a noun.

questions.question: The person ____ phone was stolen called the police.
answers: whose
questions.explanation: For possession we use **whose** (whose phone = the phone of whom).

questions.question: The town ____ I grew up is very small.
answers: where
questions.explanation: For place we use **where** (or 'that/which I grew up in').

questions.question: The teacher ____ taught us English has retired.
answers: who
questions.explanation: For people (subject) we use **who** (or that).

questions.question: Everything ____ he said was true.
answers: that
questions.explanation: After 'everything', 'something', 'anything' we use **that** (or omit). We don't use **what** here.

questions.question: The reason ____ I'm late is the traffic.
answers: why
questions.explanation: For reason we use **why** (or that).

questions.question: The hotel ____ we stayed was very comfortable.
answers: where
questions.explanation: For place we use **where** (or that/which we stayed at).

errorcorrection.title: Practice: Correct the relative pronoun (common mistakes)

questions.question: Wrong: The book what I read was very long. Correct the sentence.
answers: The book that I read was very long.
The book which I read was very long.
The book that I read was very long
The book which I read was very long
questions.explanation: Use **that** or **which** for things, never **what** after a noun.

questions.question: Wrong: The man what I met yesterday is a doctor. Correct the sentence.
answers: The man who I met yesterday is a doctor.
The man that I met yesterday is a doctor.
The man who I met yesterday is a doctor
The man that I met yesterday is a doctor
questions.explanation: Use **who** or **that** for people, not **what**.

questions.question: Wrong: This is the house what I was born in. Correct the sentence.
answers: This is the house where I was born.
This is the house that I was born in.
This is the house which I was born in.
This is the house where I was born
This is the house that I was born in
This is the house which I was born in
questions.explanation: Use **where** or **that/which I was born in**, not **what**.

questions.question: Wrong: The film what we saw was really good. Correct the sentence.
answers: The film that we saw was really good.
The film which we saw was really good.
The film that we saw was really good
The film which we saw was really good
questions.explanation: For things use **that** or **which**, never **what**.

questions.question: Wrong: The woman what works here is from Prague. Correct the sentence.
answers: The woman who works here is from Prague.
The woman that works here is from Prague.
The woman who works here is from Prague
The woman that works here is from Prague
questions.explanation: For people use **who** or **that**, not **what**.

questions.question: Wrong: I don't like the things what he said. Correct the sentence.
answers: I don't like the things that he said.
I don't like what he said.
I don't like the things that he said
I don't like what he said
questions.explanation: After 'the things' use **that** (or omit). Or use **What he said** (the thing that he said) as the object.

questions.question: Wrong: The place what we stayed was very nice. Correct the sentence.
answers: The place where we stayed was very nice.
The place that we stayed at was very nice.
The place which we stayed at was very nice.
The place where we stayed was very nice
The place that we stayed at was very nice
The place which we stayed at was very nice
questions.explanation: For place use **where** or **that/which we stayed at**, not **what**.

questions.question: Wrong: The day what we arrived was Monday. Correct the sentence.
answers: The day when we arrived was Monday.
The day that we arrived was Monday.
The day when we arrived was Monday
The day that we arrived was Monday
questions.explanation: For time use **when** or **that**, not **what**.

questions.question: Wrong: The person what helped me was very kind. Correct the sentence.
answers: The person who helped me was very kind.
The person that helped me was very kind.
The person who helped me was very kind
The person that helped me was very kind
questions.explanation: For people (subject) use **who** or **that**, not **what**.

questions.question: Wrong: That's the reason what I left. Correct the sentence.
answers: That's the reason why I left.
That is the reason why I left.
That's the reason that I left.
That is the reason that I left.
That's the reason why I left
That is the reason why I left
That's the reason that I left
That is the reason that I left
questions.explanation: For reason use **why** or **that**, not **what**.
